text: The surgeon carefully dissects the infrapyloric vessels and the right gastroepiploic vessels, ensuring preservation of the pylorus during the laparoscopic gastrectomy.
The surgeon ligates the right gastroepiploic vessels after preserving the infrapyloric vessels, using laparoscopic instruments to secure the vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully avoiding injury to the adjacent infrapyloric vessels during the laparoscopic pylorus-preserving gastrectomy.
The surgeon clears the lesser curvature near the anticipated resection area and proceeds with the resection of the distal end of the stomach, approximately five to six centimeters from the pylorus.
The surgeon performs dissection of the suprapancreatic lymph node, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues during the laparoscopic pylorus-preserving gastrectomy.
The surgeon dissects the number eight lymph node area located above the common hepatic artery, carefully separating the lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue near the lesser curvature to avoid injuring the right gastric artery during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the coronary vein and performs careful dissection around it, followed by firm ligation to secure the vessel.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the suprapancreatic lymph nodes above the splenic artery, carefully separating the lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs dissection along the left gastric artery to preserve the celiac branch of the vagus nerve, carefully navigating around these anatomical structures.
The surgeon ligates small branches entering and exiting the liver, ensuring these vessels are securely tied off.
The surgeon identifies the vagus nerve during the gastrectomy procedure, ensuring its preservation while continuing the dissection.
The surgeon dissects the white fibers located behind the left gastric artery lymph node, carefully separating them from the surrounding tissue near the left gastric artery.
The surgeon divides and ligates the left gastric artery, completing the dissection around this vessel.
The surgeon dissects the number three lymph node area, progressing towards the lesser curvature of the stomach, carefully separating the lymphatic tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon marks the proximal gastric resection line on the stomach, indicating the area where the resection will occur.
The surgeon transects the stomach, completing the division along the marked proximal gastric resection line.
A hole is created on the greater curvature side of the distal stomach, and a section of the staple line is opened, revealing the underlying tissue.
A hole is created on the proximal stomach along the greater curvature, similar to the previous incision on the distal stomach.
The surgeon places a suture on the staple line of the proximal stomach to provide traction.
The operator verifies the presence of a hole in the lesser curvature side of the distal stomach, preparing for the piercing method.
The surgeon inserts a linear stapler into both the proximal and distal stomach, aligning it with the previously created holes on the greater and lesser curvatures.

The end of the anvil of the linear stapler pierces through the hole on the lesser curvature staple line of the distal stomach, facilitating the connection between the proximal and distal stomach segments.
The surgeon gathers the stapler lines in the middle of the stomach before closing the linear stapler, ensuring alignment of the staple lines on both the proximal and distal stomach.
The surgeon cuts off the distal part of the gathered stapler lines, removing avascular sections of the stomach located between the stapler lines.
The surgeon places sutures on the greater and lesser curvatures of the gastrogastronomy site to provide traction, facilitating the alignment and stabilization of the stomach sections.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to close the entry hole of the gastrogastronomy, ensuring the avascular parts formed during the anastomosis are securely sealed.
An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to visually inspect the anastomosis site, ensuring its integrity and safety.
